examples.title: FCFS Scheduling Simulation
examples.description: Implementation of First-Come-First-Served CPU scheduling algorithm
examples.code:
def fcfs_scheduling(processes):
    # processes: list of (arrival_time, burst_time)
    n = len(processes)
    waiting_time = [0] * n
    turnaround_time = [0] * n
    
    # Sort by arrival time
    processes = sorted(processes, key=lambda x: x[0])
    
    completion_time = processes[0][0]
    for i in range(n):
        if i > 0 and processes[i][0] > completion_time:
            completion_time = processes[i][0]
        completion_time += processes[i][1]
        turnaround_time[i] = completion_time - processes[i][0]
        waiting_time[i] = turnaround_time[i] - processes[i][1]
    
    avg_waiting = sum(waiting_time) / n
    avg_turnaround = sum(turnaround_time) / n
    
    return avg_waiting, avg_turnaround

# Example: (arrival_time, burst_time)
processes = [(0, 10), (1, 5), (2, 8)]
avg_wt, avg_tat = fcfs_scheduling(processes)
print(f"Average Waiting Time: {avg_wt}")
print(f"Average Turnaround Time: {avg_tat}")
examples.explanation: This FCFS implementation sorts processes by arrival time and executes them in order. The waiting time for each process is calculated as turnaround time minus burst time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

examples.title: Round Robin Scheduling Simulation
examples.description: Implementation of Round Robin CPU scheduling with time quantum
examples.explanation: This Round Robin implementation uses a ready queue. Each process gets quantum time; if not complete, it's re-added to queue. Handles process arrivals dynamically.

examples.title: SJF Non-Preemptive Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of Shortest Job First scheduling without preemption
examples.code:
def sjf_non_preemptive(processes):
    # processes: list of (arrival_time, burst_time, process_id)
    n = len(processes)
    processes = [(p[2], p[0], p[1]) for p in processes]  # (pid, at, bt)
    
    completed = []
    current_time = 0
    ready_queue = []
    
    while len(completed) < n:
        # Add all arrived processes to ready queue
        for p in processes:
            if p[1] <= current_time and p not in ready_queue and p not in completed:
                ready_queue.append(p)
        
        if not ready_queue:
            current_time += 1
            continue
        
        # Select shortest burst time
        ready_queue.sort(key=lambda x: x[2])
        current_process = ready_queue.pop(0)
        
        current_time += current_process[2]
        completion_time = current_time
        turnaround = completion_time - current_process[1]
        waiting = turnaround - current_process[2]
        
        completed.append((current_process[0], completion_time, turnaround, waiting))
    
    completed.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    return completed

# Example: (arrival, burst, pid)
processes = [(0, 7, 1), (2, 4, 2), (4, 1, 3), (5, 6, 4)]
results = sjf_non_preemptive(processes)
for r in results:
    print(f"P{r[0]}: Completion={r[1]}, Turnaround={r[2]}, Waiting={r[3]}")
examples.explanation: SJF selects the process with smallest burst time from available processes. It provides optimal average waiting time but cannot predict burst times in practice.

examples.title: Priority Scheduling with Aging
examples.description: Implementation of priority scheduling that incorporates aging to prevent starvation
examples.explanation: This implementation shows priority scheduling where waiting processes gain priority over time. For every 5 units of waiting, priority improves by 1 (up to max 10), preventing indefinite starvation.
